title: Meet the CES Medical team
caption:
Cataracts. Eyelid surgery. Glaucoma. Dry eye.

Behind every condition is a question someone has been sitting on for too long.

We are here to answer them. In Kent, close to home, with the same consultants and the same standards whether you come to us through the NHS or privately.

title: Do cataracts only affect older people?
caption:
Cataracts are most common after 60, but they can develop at any age, triggered by injury, certain medications or underlying health conditions.

If your vision feels cloudy or like you are looking through a smudged lens, it is worth getting checked. Whatever your age.

title: What is a cataract?
caption:
That foggy, hazy feeling when your vision is not quite right? It could be a cataract.

In 60 seconds, we break down exactly what is happening inside your eye, what it feels like and what treating it involves.

Visit cespatientinformation.co.uk to find out more or ask our patient AI any question about your care.
notes: Export as a separate native file for Facebook. Do not repost from Instagram. No watermarks.

title: Meet Mr Nick Kopsachilis
caption:
Our consultants work across both NHS and private pathways. The same hands. The same standards. Regardless of how you come to us.

Mr Nick Kopsachilis is a Consultant Ophthalmic Surgeon specialising in corneal and cataract surgery. He holds an MD, PhD and a Postgraduate Diploma in Cataract and Refractive Surgery, awarded with distinction. He trained in Munich, at Moorfields Eye Hospital and at King's College London before joining CES Medical.

He sees patients at Headcorn, Pantiles, Chatham and Southborough.

title: What is a monofocal lens?
caption:
A monofocal lens corrects vision at one distance. Most people choose distance vision and use reading glasses for close-up work.

It is reliable, well-established and the lens included in standard cataract surgery. Not every patient needs a more complex lens. What matters is that the choice fits your life.

Swipe through to see who monofocal lenses suit best.

cta.label: See if a consultation is right for you

title: NHS vs private cataract surgery, what actually changes?
caption:
NHS cataract waiting times have stretched in many areas of Kent. Going private with CES means you can typically be assessed within two weeks and have surgery shortly after.

The consultants are the same. The clinical standards are the same. What changes is the timeline, the time at consultation and the range of lens options available to you.

If you have been on an NHS list and want to understand your options, a conversation costs nothing.

title: A morning at our Chatham surgical centre
caption:
Theatre prep, the team checking the day list, the first patient through the door.

Every morning here starts before any patient arrives. We wanted to show you what that looks like.

title: Could you be glasses-free after cataract surgery?
caption:
Multifocal lenses can give you vision at multiple distances. Many patients find they no longer need glasses after surgery.

They are not right for everyone. Some people notice haloes or reduced contrast in low light. We will always tell you honestly whether a multifocal lens suits your eyes and your lifestyle before recommending one.

Swipe through for what to expect.

title: Which cataract lens is right for you?
caption:
Monofocal, multifocal, EDOF, toric. The choice can feel overwhelming.

The honest answer is: the right lens depends on your eyes, your lifestyle, your hobbies and your expectations. Not on what looks best on a comparison chart.

Swipe through our breakdown. Then bring your questions to the consultation.

title: Is your eyelid problem medical or cosmetic?
caption:
Drooping upper eyelids that cut into your field of vision are a medical issue, not a cosmetic one. That distinction matters for how the procedure is approached and, in many cases, whether your insurance covers it.

Here is how we tell the difference at consultation.

title: That lump on your eyelid, when should you get it checked?
caption:
Most eyelid lesions are harmless. But some need a closer look.

If something has appeared, changed shape, bled or persisted for more than a few weeks, it is worth a specialist assessment. Our oculoplastic team can usually see you within a week.

The first step is always a conversation.

title: What happens between cases in our theatre?
caption:
Every patient goes into a theatre that has been fully reset and sterilised.

It is invisible to most people. But it is one of the most important things we do, and we wanted to show you how it works.

title: Five things people get wrong about laser eye surgery
caption:
"It is risky." "It is painful." "It needs redoing in ten years."

These are the five most common things people tell us before a conversation about laser vision correction. Here is what the evidence actually says.

title: Do you have astigmatism as well as cataracts?
caption:
If you have astigmatism alongside a cataract, a toric lens can correct both at the same time. Many patients find they no longer need distance glasses afterwards.

It is worth raising at your consultation if it applies to you. The difference in outcome can be significant.

The honest answer: we show you everything that helps you feel ready. The things that are better explained face to face, we leave for the consultation.

title: What does FRCOphth mean for you as a patient?
caption:
Our consultant surgeons carry FRCOphth after their names. It stands for Fellow of the Royal College of Ophthalmologists, the highest professional qualification for eye surgeons in the UK.

It is not honorary. It requires years of supervised surgical training, written and practical examinations and ongoing revalidation. Our lead consultants hold it. Here is what that means in practical terms for your care.

title: What does recovery from eyelid surgery actually look like?
caption:
Day one: bruising and swelling are normal and expected.
Day seven: stitches typically come out.
Week two: most people are back to everyday activities.
Week six: the final result settles.

Here is what to expect honestly, including the things that vary between patients.

title: Your insurance may cover more than you think
caption:
Bupa, AXA Health, Vitality, WPA, many UK private medical insurance policies cover cataract surgery and functional eyelid procedures.

Our team can check your cover before you commit to anything. No obligation, no pressure. We handle most of the pre-authorisation paperwork once you are ready to proceed.

title: Glaucoma rarely gives any warning
caption:
Glaucoma rarely has symptoms in its early stages. It damages the optic nerve gradually and, without regular monitoring, the first sign is often vision that cannot be restored.

That is what makes regular eye examinations so important, particularly after 40. We diagnose and manage glaucoma at all our centres, including laser treatment where appropriate.

title: A morning with our oculoplastic consultant
caption:
From the first consultation of the day to the operating list, a behind-the-scenes morning with our oculoplastic consultant at our Tunbridge Wells clinic.

The work goes well beyond eyelids. But eyelids are where most patients first meet us.

title: Why does the second eye matter as much as the first?
caption:
Many people have one cataract treated and then put off the second. It is understandable. But depth perception, balance and night driving all rely on both eyes working together.

Here is why the second eye usually matters more than people expect.

title: Is your dry eye affecting your daily life?
caption:
Persistent dry eye can affect your reading, your screen work and your driving. For many patients, drops alone are not enough.

We offer IPL (Intense Pulsed Light) treatment alongside conventional management for patients whose dry eye is not responding to standard care. It is worth asking about if you have been managing symptoms for a while.

A short walk-through of our Headcorn site, diagnostics, consulting rooms, theatres and the team that makes it all run.

If you have an appointment coming up at Headcorn and are not sure what to expect, this is it.

title: Multifocal lenses and varifocal glasses are not the same thing
caption:
Varifocals are glasses. Multifocal lenses are implants placed inside your eye during cataract surgery.

The confusion is common and important to clear up before your consultation. Swipe through for the difference.

For private appointments at CES Medical, you can refer yourself directly. No GP letter needed.

Our team will guide you through what we need from your optician and book you in with the right consultant for your condition.

James was referred to us with an acute eye condition. He was nervous and in pain.

title: Why does eyelid surgery need an ophthalmologist?
caption:
Your eyelids protect your eyes. Surgery on them sits closer to ophthalmology than to aesthetics.

An oculoplastic surgeon is an ophthalmologist with additional subspecialty training. They understand the anatomy of the eye, the tear film and the risk to vision in a way a cosmetic surgeon does not. That distinction matters for both functional and cosmetic eyelid work.

title: Why precise measurement matters in cataract surgery
caption:
Precise lens selection starts with precise measurement.

The Zeiss IOLMaster 700 gives us highly accurate biometry data before surgery. The reason that matters is straightforward: if the measurement is slightly wrong, the lens power will be slightly wrong, and your vision after surgery will be less predictable.

We use it because precision at the planning stage directly affects the outcome.

title: If you have diabetes, your eyes need regular attention
caption:
Diabetes can affect the small blood vessels in your retina, sometimes years before you notice anything.

Annual diabetic eye screening is not optional maintenance. It is how sight loss is prevented. We diagnose and manage diabetic retinopathy alongside our wider retina services.

title: Has your cataract come back? It almost certainly has not.
caption:
Some patients develop cloudy vision months or years after successful cataract surgery. It is usually because the lens capsule has gradually thickened, not because the cataract has returned.

A short YAG laser procedure clears it, often in under ten minutes. No incision. Outpatient. The improvement in vision is usually immediate.

title: Meet Mr Kashif Qureshi
caption:
Mr Kashif Qureshi is a Consultant Ophthalmic Surgeon with over 25 years of experience in cataract surgery and medical retina.

He trained at Guy's and St Thomas's Hospitals in London, with subspecialty fellowships in Medical Retina at Manchester Royal Eye Hospital and Moorfields Eye Hospital. For four years he served as UK Lead for Revalidation for all ophthalmologists, overseeing the professional standards every eye surgeon in the country is held to.

He is one of a very small number of surgeons in the UK offering YAG laser vitreolysis for vitreous floaters and 2RT nanopulse laser treatment for dry AMD.

He sees patients at Pantiles and Headcorn.

title: Meet Mr Syed Shahid
caption:
Mr Syed Shahid is a Consultant Ophthalmologist and Vitreoretinal Surgeon, and one of the very few vitreoretinal surgeons serving patients in Kent.

He trained at Moorfields Eye Hospital, where he completed an 18-month vitreoretinal surgery fellowship. He is the Clinical Lead for the Kent and Medway Diabetic Eye Screening Programme, overseeing diabetic eye care across the region.

His specialist work covers retinal detachment, macular hole, epiretinal membrane, complex cataract surgery and premium lens implants. He is rated 4.93 on Doctify from 127 verified patient reviews.

He sees patients at Southborough.

title: Meet Elion Hyseni, Founder and CEO
caption:
Elion Hyseni founded CES Medical in 2018 with one idea: that specialist eye care should be available locally, without compromise.

He is a Consultant Clinical Optometrist with advanced qualifications in clinical optometry, clinical neuroscience and healthcare management. He holds a professional certificate in glaucoma and an independent prescribing qualification. Before CES Medical, he spent years watching patients in Kent travel to London for specialist care that could and should have been available closer to home.

He now leads a team of over 25 consultants and clinicians across four primary locations, serving both NHS and private patients across Kent.

title: Mr Kopsachilis on corneal transplantation
caption:

Mr Nick Kopsachilis, Consultant Ophthalmic Surgeon and corneal specialist, explains what corneal transplantation involves, who it is for and why his PhD research directly informs how he approaches the most complex cases.
videoReference: NK-C11 + corneal transplantation addition (see production notes)
notes: Additional interview question required: 'What is a corneal transplant and who needs one?' Answer guidance: the cornea is the clear window at the front of the eye. When it becomes scarred, swollen or misshapen, vision is significantly affected. A corneal transplant replaces the damaged tissue with donor tissue. Types include DMEK and DSEK for Fuchs' dystrophy. Mr Kopsachilis performs all types and his doctoral research into corneal cell behaviour directly informs how he plans each procedure.

The difference between a functional and a cosmetic eyelid procedure is not always obvious. Even to the patient.

caption:

By the time a patient notices a change in their peripheral vision, some damage has already occurred. And unlike a cataract, that damage cannot be reversed. It can only be slowed.

title: 2RT nanopulse laser for dry AMD
caption:
There is a laser procedure for dry AMD that almost no one in the UK knows exists.

Dry age-related macular degeneration affects the central vision. For most patients, the advice has historically been: manage your risk factors and wait. The wet form has treatments. The dry form, until recently, has had very few.

The 2RT nanopulse laser changes that picture for some patients. One of our consultants at CES Medical is one of a very small number of specialists in the UK with experience using it.

Swipe through for what 2RT is, who it suits and what the evidence currently shows.

How often do you have conversations with dry AMD patients where the available options feel inadequate? We would be interested to understand whether this is a gap GPs and optometrists encounter regularly.
linkedInHook: There is a laser procedure for dry AMD that almost no one in the UK knows exists.

title: Why I started CES Medical
caption:
I started CES Medical in 2018 because I kept watching the same thing happen.

A patient would come in for a routine appointment. I would identify something that needed specialist attention. I would refer them. And then I would hear back, months later, that they were still waiting.

Not because the system did not care. Because the specialist capacity in Kent was not where the patients were.

I had trained in optometry, in clinical neuroscience, in glaucoma management. I understood what good looked like. And I knew that the patients in Chatham and Headcorn and Tunbridge Wells deserved the same access to it as someone who could get themselves to London.

So we built it here instead.

Six years later, I still think about that gap. Every new clinic, every new consultant, every new service is an answer to the same question: why should where you live determine the quality of your eye care.

It should not. That is what we are trying to fix.

What made you choose the work you are in? I am genuinely curious whether this kind of frustration is what drives most people in healthcare.
linkedInHook: I started CES Medical in 2018 because I kept watching the same thing happen.

title: What consultant-led actually means
caption:
Consultant-led is a phrase that gets used a lot in private healthcare. I want to be specific about what it means at CES Medical because it is not a marketing phrase for us.

It means the consultant who sees you at your first appointment is the consultant who performs your surgery.

That matters for several reasons. The surgeon knows exactly what they found at assessment. They made the decision to operate based on what they saw personally. They know your specific anatomy before they enter the theatre. There is no handover, no interpretation of another clinician's notes, no adjustment on the day.

In a non-consultant-led model, assessment and surgery can be performed by different clinicians. That is common. It is not necessarily wrong. But for procedures on something as precise as the human eye, continuity of care has real clinical value.

This is why we built CES Medical around it. Not because it sounded better. Because it produces better outcomes.

Does this distinction matter to you in how you refer patients or choose providers? I would be interested in whether GPs and optometrists weigh this when making decisions.
linkedInHook: Consultant-led is a phrase that gets used a lot in private healthcare.

title: The real reason patients delay eye treatment
caption:
People assume patients delay eye treatment because of cost or fear of surgery.

In my experience, the most common reason is much simpler. They do not know if they are bad enough yet.

They have adapted to their vision changing gradually. They have stopped driving at night and told themselves it is a preference, not a problem. They have moved to larger print and told themselves they just need stronger glasses. They have quietly accepted a narrowing world and called it ageing.

They are not avoiding us. They do not know they need us.

This changes how we communicate. We do not spend much time trying to convince people who are ready to act. We spend most of our time helping people recognise that the question they have been sitting on for two years is worth asking out loud.

That is why we publish what we publish. Not to sell. To give someone permission to pick up the phone.

Does this match what you see in your practice? How do you handle the patient who has been managing fine for much longer than they should have been?
linkedInHook: People assume patients delay eye treatment because of cost or fear of surgery.

title: Why CES treats both NHS and private patients
caption:
Some people ask why we treat NHS patients when we are a private group.

The answer is straightforward. Eye disease does not discriminate by income. Cataracts, glaucoma and diabetic retinopathy affect people across every demographic in Kent. We believe specialist ophthalmology should be available to all of them.

Our NHS patients are seen by the same consultants, in the same facilities, to the same clinical standards as our private patients. The difference is access speed and lens choice for cataract patients. Not the quality of the surgeon or the surgery.

We made this a founding principle of CES Medical and we have not moved from it. Commercial pressure will always push a private group towards its most profitable patient group. We resist that because we think it is the wrong way to build healthcare.

I am interested in whether this is a common view in the sector or whether most people see NHS and private as necessarily separate.
linkedInHook: Some people ask why we treat NHS patients when we are a private group.

title: How I chose the consultants at CES Medical
caption:
People assume clinical decisions are made on reputation and referral networks.

For me it started with a different question: what kind of doctor talks to their patient during surgery.

I have seen enough operative environments to know that technical skill is table stakes at the level we were recruiting. What differentiates surgeons at the top of their field is how they treat the person on the table. Whether they explain what is happening. Whether they notice anxiety and respond to it. Whether the patient feels held as well as treated.

Every consultant at CES Medical was chosen partly on that basis. Not because it is soft or commercial. Because a patient having surgery on their eye is in one of the most vulnerable positions they will ever be in. The person doing that surgery should know it.

How do you evaluate clinical culture when you are building a team? I am genuinely curious whether others use similar criteria or whether this is an unusual way to make those decisions.

title: What a clinical optometrist sees that a standard eye test misses
caption:
A GP once told me he did not know there was a difference between a clinical optometrist and a high street optician until a patient of his came back from a CES assessment with a glaucoma diagnosis that had been missed at three previous routine tests.

The difference is training and scope. A community optometrist checks your prescription, screens for obvious pathology and refers when something is outside their competence. A specialist clinical optometrist with independent prescribing qualifications can diagnose, initiate treatment, monitor complex conditions and manage the patient longitudinally without a GP or ophthalmologist in the loop for every decision.

I hold a professional certificate in glaucoma and an independent prescribing qualification. That means I can assess a patient presenting with raised IOP, make a clinical decision about whether they need treatment, initiate drops if appropriate and monitor them over time. That is a fundamentally different scope to a standard optometry appointment.

This matters in a system under pressure. Not every patient with a glaucoma suspect result needs to sit on an ophthalmology waiting list. Some of them can be safely managed in a community optometry setting with the right qualifications. Others need onward referral. The skill is knowing which is which.

Does this distinction come up in your practice? I am curious how many GPs are aware of the difference in scope between optometrists at different levels.

title: Why corneal transplantation is one of the most technically demanding procedures in ophthalmology
caption:
Corneal transplantation has changed dramatically in the past decade. We have moved away from full-thickness grafts towards selective lamellar procedures that transplant only the diseased layer of the cornea rather than the entire structure.

DMEK (Descemet Membrane Endothelial Keratoplasty) is the most technically refined of these. The tissue being transplanted is a single cell layer, approximately 10 to 15 microns thick. To put that in context, a human hair is around 70 microns wide. The graft has no structural rigidity. It must be inserted into the eye through a small incision, unfolded without damage and positioned precisely against the recipient cornea using an air bubble.

The learning curve is steep. Outcomes in experienced hands are significantly better than in lower-volume centres. For patients with Fuchs' endothelial dystrophy or bullous keratopathy, this distinction matters.

I completed my doctoral research on corneal endothelial cell biology before specialising in transplantation. That foundation changes how I think about every operative decision, not just the technical steps but the cellular logic behind them.

If you are an optometrist or GP seeing patients with progressive corneal oedema or unexplained visual decline that does not respond to refraction, I am happy to discuss cases informally before a formal referral is made.

What is your experience of the referral pathway for corneal conditions? I am interested in whether the current system is identifying these patients early enough.

title: Multifocal lens implants: what patients are not always told before they decide
caption:
Multifocal intraocular lenses are one of the most significant advances in cataract surgery of the past twenty years. For appropriately selected patients, the chance of being completely glasses-free after surgery is real and the outcomes are excellent.

But I want to be direct about something that does not always get enough weight in patient-facing communications.

Multifocal lenses divide light. They create multiple focal points simultaneously. For most patients, the visual cortex adapts and the brain learns to select the relevant focus for the task. For some patients, it does not. Those patients experience halos, glare and reduced contrast sensitivity, particularly in low light. Night driving can become genuinely difficult.

The patient who asks me will I need glasses after my cataract surgery is asking the right question. But the question I need to answer first is are you the right patient for a lens that might eliminate glasses but might also change how you see at night.

That requires an honest conversation about lifestyle, expectations, driving habits, occupational visual demands and the clinical characteristics of their eye. It is not a five-minute decision.

At CES we take that conversation seriously because recommending a multifocal lens to the wrong patient creates a problem that is very difficult to reverse.

What do you observe in patients who have had multifocal lenses fitted elsewhere and are presenting with visual complaints? I am interested in whether this is a pattern others are seeing.
linkedInHook: Multifocal intraocular lenses are one of the most significant advances in cataract surgery of the past twenty years.
notes: Mr Kopsachilis to review and post from his personal account.

title: What dry AMD patients are not being told
caption:
When a patient receives a dry AMD diagnosis, the standard conversation has historically covered three things: lifestyle modification, AREDS supplementation and monitoring.

That conversation is not wrong. But I think it has become a ceiling that stops both clinicians and patients from asking the next question.

Dry AMD is not a static condition. It progresses. And for patients who are watching their central vision slowly narrow over years, being told there is nothing to treat is a significant burden to carry.

I have spent a considerable part of my career in medical retina, and I am cautious about overclaiming for emerging treatments. The evidence base for some newer approaches is still developing. But I also think patients have a right to know that the picture is more complex than monitor and wait.

If you have patients with early to intermediate dry AMD who have been told there are no options, a conversation with a medical retina specialist who is familiar with the full range of current approaches is worth having. Not because every patient will be a candidate for something different. But because some will be, and they deserve to know.

I am not making specific treatment claims in a LinkedIn post. What I am saying is that the referral threshold for a specialist opinion on dry AMD should be lower than it currently is in many settings.

Has this come up in your practice? I would be interested in whether GPs and optometrists feel they have enough information to have this conversation with their patients.
linkedInHook: When a patient receives a dry AMD diagnosis, the standard conversation has historically covered three things.
notes: Mr Qureshi to review and post from his personal account. This post deliberately avoids naming specific treatments to stay within GMC advertising standards.

title: What the revalidation process for ophthalmologists actually involves
caption:
I served as Co-Lead for Revalidation at the Royal College of Ophthalmologists for four years. I want to explain what revalidation actually involves because I think patients and referring clinicians often have a vague sense of it as a box-ticking exercise. It is not.

Every consultant in the UK undergoes annual appraisal and five-yearly revalidation. To revalidate, a consultant must demonstrate: continuing professional development across the breadth of their practice, clinical outcome data, multi-source feedback from colleagues and patients, a record of significant events and complaints with evidence of reflection and learning and confirmation that they have no health or conduct issues affecting their practice.

The appraisal process requires a consultant to sit with an appraiser and work through all of this documentation in detail. It is not a rubber stamp.

When I led this process at the Royal College level, I was responsible for ensuring that the standards applied to every ophthalmologist in the country were meaningful. That experience changed how I think about my own practice. You cannot set rigorous standards for others and then hold yourself to a lower bar.

When a patient asks me how they know their surgeon is safe to practice, revalidation is part of the answer. Not the whole answer. But a real and important part.

Does the revalidation process come up in conversations with patients when they are choosing a provider? I am curious whether this is something patients are beginning to ask about directly.
linkedInHook: I served as Co-Lead for Revalidation at the Royal College of Ophthalmologists for four years.

title: Retinal detachment: the window that most patients do not know exists
caption:
The visual outcome from retinal detachment surgery is substantially better when the macula is still attached at the time of the operation.

Once the detachment reaches the macula, the central vision is affected. Surgery can still repair the retina, but the recovery of central vision is less predictable and often incomplete.

The challenge is that patients experiencing the warning signs of retinal detachment frequently do not understand the urgency. A sudden shower of floaters and flashes is frightening, but it does not feel like an emergency in the way that chest pain does. People wait to see if it settles. They book an appointment with their optician. They look it up online.

The signs that need same-day assessment: a sudden significant increase in floaters, flashes of light particularly in peripheral vision, or a shadow or curtain moving across the visual field. Any of these, particularly in combination, need to be assessed urgently. Not in a few days. That day.

For GPs: a patient presenting with these symptoms should be directed to an eye casualty department immediately, not referred in the routine pathway.

I am one of a small number of vitreoretinal surgeons in Kent. I see the outcomes difference that speed makes. It is real and it is significant.

What is your experience of patients presenting late with retinal symptoms? I would like to understand where the delay most commonly occurs.
linkedInHook: The visual outcome from retinal detachment surgery is substantially better when the macula is still attached at the time of the operation.

title: What a vitreoretinal surgeon actually does and why having one in Kent matters
caption:
Vitreoretinal surgery covers the back of the eye: the retina, the vitreous gel and the structures that support them.

The conditions that fall within this subspecialty include retinal detachment, macular hole, epiretinal membrane, vitreous haemorrhage, diabetic vitreoretinal disease and some forms of trauma. These are not common conditions in absolute terms. But when they occur, they are often sight-threatening and time-sensitive.

Vitreoretinal surgery requires subspecialty training beyond general ophthalmology. I completed an 18-month fellowship at Moorfields Eye Hospital specifically in this area, where the volume and complexity of cases provides an exposure that is difficult to replicate elsewhere.

The reason this matters geographically is that vitreoretinal surgeons are not evenly distributed. In many parts of the country, patients needing this surgery travel significant distances or wait for capacity at major centres. For time-sensitive conditions like retinal detachment, that waiting is not always clinically safe.

Being based in Kent with this subspecialty means that patients in Southborough, Chatham and across the region have access to this level of care closer to home.

If you have patients you are uncertain about referring, or conditions that you think might benefit from a vitreoretinal opinion, I am happy to discuss informally before a formal referral is made.

What is your current pathway for suspected retinal conditions? I would like to understand what the referral experience looks like from a GP or optometrist perspective in Kent.

title: The moment you realise your vision has changed
caption:
There is usually a specific moment when it becomes undeniable.

For some people it is a car journey at night when the oncoming headlights suddenly seem unbearable. For others it is trying to read a menu in a restaurant and holding it further and further away. For others it is a grandchild pointing at something in the distance that they simply cannot see.

These moments are not signs of getting old. They are often signs of a cataract developing.

Cataracts are gradual. They are also very treatable. If you have had one of those moments recently, it is worth a conversation.

Call us on 01732 757771. No obligation, no rush.

Have you had a moment like this? We would love to hear from you in the comments.

title: What surgery day actually looks like
caption:
We hear the same fears every week.

"Will I be awake?" Yes, but patients do not feel pain. Local anaesthetic drops are used throughout.
"Will I see what is happening?" No. Patients see light and movement but not the procedure itself.
"What if I move my eye?" The team is trained for this. It is more common than you think and it does not affect the outcome.
"How long does it take?" Around 15 to 20 minutes per eye.
"Can my partner come?" Yes. They can wait in our reception and be with you immediately after.

Surgery on your eye is frightening to think about. Most patients tell us it was much more manageable than they expected.

If you have a question that is keeping you from making the first call, ask us.

Call 01732 757771 or comment below and we will come back to you.

title: Five signs your parent might have a cataract they have not mentioned
caption:
If your mum or dad is over 65, there is a reasonable chance they have a cataract developing that they have not told you about. Not because they are hiding it. Because it happened so gradually they did not notice.

Here are five things to look for.

title: Drooping eyelids and vision
caption:
A drooping upper eyelid is not always a cosmetic concern.

When the lid drops far enough to cut into your field of vision, it becomes a medical condition. Patients describe it as: feeling like they are always looking from under a heavy brow, difficulty reading for long periods, needing to lift their chin to see clearly.

title: Glaucoma and family history
caption:
If one of your parents had glaucoma, your risk of developing it is three to four times higher than average.

Glaucoma has no symptoms in its early stages. It damages your peripheral vision slowly, over years, without pain. By the time most people notice something is wrong, some damage has already occurred.

The good news is that caught early, glaucoma is very manageable. Drops, laser treatment and surgery are all available depending on the stage and type.

If you have a family history of glaucoma and have not had a full eye examination in the past two years, that is where to start.

Your optician can screen for early signs. If they refer you to us, we can usually see you within two weeks.

title: Lens options for cataract surgery: the honest comparison
caption:
Monofocal. Multifocal. EDOF. Toric.

If you are researching cataract surgery, the lens conversation can feel overwhelming. Here is an honest breakdown. Save this before your consultation.
notes: Carousel 7 slides. Slide 1: Which cataract lens is right for you? The honest breakdown. Slide 2: Monofocal lens. Corrects one distance (usually distance vision). Most patients still need reading glasses. Most established option. Included in standard surgery. Slide 3: Multifocal lens. Corrects multiple distances. Many patients glasses-free. Not for everyone: some experience haloes or glare at night. Requires clinical assessment to confirm suitability. Slide 4: EDOF (Extended Depth of Focus). Extended intermediate range. Fewer haloes than full multifocal. May still need reading glasses for fine print. A middle-ground option for many patients. Slide 5: Toric lens. Corrects distance vision and astigmatism at the same time. Does not address reading vision separately. Slide 6: Multifocal vs varifocal glasses. Not the same thing. A multifocal IOL is a surgical implant inside the eye. A varifocal is spectacle lenses. Important distinction before your consultation. Slide 7: The right lens is the one that fits your life, not the one that looks best on a chart. That conversation happens at your consultation. cespatientinformation.co.uk. High save potential. title: Glaucoma: everything you need to know if you have just been diagnosed
caption:
If you or someone you love has just been told they have glaucoma, this is for you.

It is a condition that sounds frightening and is frequently misunderstood. Swipe through for what it actually means, what the options are and what life with managed glaucoma looks like.

Save this.
notes: Carousel 9 slides. Slide 1: Just diagnosed with glaucoma? Here is what it actually means. Slide 2: Glaucoma is not one condition. It is a group of conditions that damage the optic nerve, usually through raised pressure inside the eye. Slide 3: The most important thing to know: caught early, glaucoma is very manageable. The damage cannot be reversed but it can be slowed significantly. Slide 4: Why you may not have noticed symptoms. Glaucoma typically affects peripheral vision first. The brain compensates so well that most people do not notice until a screening test picks it up. Slide 5: What management looks like. Eye drops (most common first step). SLT laser (five-minute outpatient procedure, very effective at lowering pressure). Surgery for advanced cases. Slide 6: About SLT laser at CES Medical. Selective Laser Trabeculoplasty is a quick, well-tolerated procedure that lowers eye pressure without daily drop compliance. Many patients prefer it as a first-line option. Slide 7: What monitoring involves. Regular appointments to check eye pressure, optic nerve imaging and visual field testing. Usually every six to twelve months depending on stability. Slide 8: Does it mean I will go blind? For most patients managed appropriately from diagnosis: no. Sight loss from glaucoma is largely preventable with treatment. Slide 9: We see glaucoma patients at Chatham and Headcorn. cespatientinformation.co.uk or call 01732 757771. High save and share potential. Designed to be shared by newly diagnosed patients with family members. No alarm, no overclaiming on outcomes. title: Dry eye: why it is being under-treated
caption:
Dry eye is one of the most under-treated conditions in ophthalmology.

Not because it is rare. Because most people manage it with drops for years without knowing there are other options.

Swipe through for what we actually offer dry eye patients at CES Medical.
notes: Carousel 7 slides. Slide 1: Dry eye. More than just irritation. Slide 2: What dry eye actually is. The eye does not produce enough tears, or produces tears of insufficient quality. The result: irritation, redness, blurred vision and difficulty with screens, reading and driving. Slide 3: Why drops alone are often not enough. Drops provide temporary relief by supplementing tears. They do not address the underlying cause, usually meibomian gland dysfunction (blocked glands at the eyelid margin). Slide 4: IPL treatment for dry eye. Intense Pulsed Light therapy targets the meibomian glands directly, improving the quality of the oil layer of the tear film. Clinically proven. Available at CES Medical. Slide 5: Who is a candidate for IPL? Patients who have been using drops for months or years without sustained relief. Patients with meibomian gland dysfunction confirmed on examination. Patients whose dry eye significantly affects screen work, driving or reading. Slide 6: What IPL treatment involves. A series of sessions, typically three to four, spaced a few weeks apart. Non-invasive. No downtime. Most patients notice improvement after the second session. Slide 7: If drops have not been enough, IPL is worth asking about. cespatientinformation.co.uk or call 01732 757771. Include a close-up clinical image of a slit lamp examination for visual credibility. Non-alarming. title: Retinal detachment: warning signs you should know
caption:
A sudden increase in floaters. Flashes of light at the edge of your vision. A shadow or curtain moving across your sight.

These are the warning signs of retinal detachment. It is a medical emergency. Do not wait for a routine appointment.

Mr Syed Shahid is a Moorfields-trained vitreoretinal surgeon based in Kent, one of very few in the region. If you or someone you know experiences these symptoms, seek urgent eye care the same day.

For urgent private assessment: 01732 757771
notes: Single image. Clean, calm, serious but not alarming. Dark teal background. White text. Key symptoms listed clearly. Phone number prominent. This post could prevent sight loss. Treat it accordingly.

title: Keratoconus: what it is and when to seek help
caption:
Keratoconus is a progressive condition where the cornea gradually thins and bulges forward. It typically starts in the teens or twenties and can significantly affect vision if left unmanaged.

The good news: caught early, it is very treatable. Corneal crosslinking can halt progression. Advanced cases may need a corneal transplant, a procedure Mr Nick Kopsachilis performs at Headcorn.

If your glasses prescription keeps changing or your vision is distorted even with correction, it is worth a specialist assessment.

Visit cespatientinformation.co.uk
notes: Carousel 6 slides. Slide 1: What is keratoconus? Slide 2: The cornea is the clear window at the front of the eye. In keratoconus it gradually thins and changes shape, distorting vision in a way that glasses and contact lenses cannot fully correct. Slide 3: Who does it affect? Most commonly people in their teens and twenties. Often hereditary. More common in patients who rub their eyes frequently. Slide 4: How is it managed? Corneal crosslinking strengthens the cornea and halts progression. It works best when caught early. Slide 5: When is a corneal transplant needed? In advanced cases where the cornea is significantly misshapen, a transplant may be the right option. Mr Kopsachilis performs DMEK and other transplant procedures at Headcorn. Slide 6: If your vision keeps changing despite updated glasses, ask your optician to check for keratoconus. Early referral makes a significant difference. cespatientinformation.co.uk

title: Dry eye and IPL: when drops are not enough
caption:
If you have been using dry eye drops for months without lasting relief, you may not be getting to the cause.

Most persistent dry eye is caused by meibomian gland dysfunction, blocked glands at the eyelid margin that affect the oil layer of your tears. Drops supplement the tears. IPL treats the glands directly.

We offer Intense Pulsed Light treatment for dry eye at CES Medical. It is non-invasive, takes around 15 minutes per session and most patients need three to four sessions spaced a few weeks apart.

Call 01732 757771 to ask whether IPL is suitable for you.
notes: Carousel 6 slides. Slide 1: Dry eye and IPL: when drops are not enough. Slide 2: Persistent dry eye is rarely just about tear volume. The most common cause is meibomian gland dysfunction, blocked glands that affect the quality of the oil layer of your tears. Slide 3: What drops do. They supplement the tears and provide temporary relief. They do not address the blocked glands that cause the problem. Slide 4: What IPL does. Intense Pulsed Light targets the meibomian glands directly, improving the oil layer and reducing the cycle of inflammation. Clinically proven. Non-invasive. Slide 5: Who it suits. Patients who have been using drops for months or years without sustained improvement. Patients with confirmed meibomian gland dysfunction. Patients whose dry eye significantly affects screen work, driving or reading. Slide 6: Three to four sessions, spaced a few weeks apart. Most patients notice improvement after the second session. Call 01732 757771 to find out if IPL is right for you.
